coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen/sulfur Co-doping strategy to synthesis green-yellow emitting carbon dots derived from xylose: Toward application in pH sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Pei
Zhu, Ziqi
Chen, Weimin
Luo, Min
Zhang, Wei
Zhang, Tao
Chen, Minzhi
Zhou, Xiaoyan
coredata.dc:description: It is still a great challenge to synthesize carbohydrate-derived carbon dots (CDs) with long-wavelength emissions via a green and facile strategy. Herein, novel xylose-derived CDs with green-yellow emission (i.e., GY-CDs) were synthesized due to the doping effect of S and N elements, using a one-pot microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The maximum emission located at 533 nm under 460 nm excitation, and the absolute quantum yield was as high as 42.44%. To unveil the emission mechanism, green and blue emissive xylose-derived CDs doped with N were also synthesized, respectively. Comparative study and various structural characterizations revealed that introducing protonated amino groups and sulfur-related moieties can narrow the energy gaps within surface states and contribute to the green-yellow emission. Further, the GY-CDs not only displayed transmutation of luminescent centers when the solution pH was above 6, but also exhibited ratiometric fluorescence changes in the pH range of 6–10 under 360 and 460 nm excitation, such distinctive dual-mode response, which together with excellent anti-interference, good water solubility, and outstanding photostability enables the prepared GY-CDs to be potential pH nanosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Xylose
Green-yellow emission
Microwave method
Surface state
Ratiometric pH sensors

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for 333 Talents Project in Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 31870549 ], the Jiangsu Nature Science Foundation [grant number BK20161524 ]; the Program for 333 Talents Project in Jiangsu Province [grant Number BRA2016381 ]; the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province [grant number KYCX17_839 ]; the Doctorate Fellowship Foundation of Nanjing Forestry University (2017); and the Advanced Analysis and Testing Center of Nanjing Forestry University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of GY-CDs. (b) Representative TEM image, (c) SAED, and (d) HRTEM image. These morphological observations reveal that the GY-CDs showed wide size distribution with an average diameter of 8.43 nm, and were also characterized by crystalline nature. (e) Raman spectral features, the D band at around 1350 cm−1 and the G band at around 1580 cm−1, are indicated. (f) EDS pattern is given for illustrating the presence of C, O, N, and S elements on the surfaces of GY-CDs. (g) FTIR characterization for the GY-CDs indicates their surface-contained different N-, and S-related groups.

(a) Emission spectra of the GY-CDs were measured at various excitation wavelength in the range of 360–480 nm, illustrating that the GY-CDs exhibited slight excitation-dependent emission and predominant excitation-independent emission. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and PL excitation spectrum were recorded from the GY-CDs, showing large overlap in the long-wavelength region. (c) The recorded fluorescence decay curve of GY-CDs, and the fitting result reveal the existence of rapid-decay lifetime (τ1) and slow-decay lifetime (τ2).

(a) High-resolution XPS C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p spectra of the GY-CDs. The deconvolution of XPS C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra for (b) G-CDs and (c) B-CDs. Comparisons of the XPS data suggest that the GY-CDs and G-CDs were possessed of plentiful amino groups on their surfaces, but in the absence of CO moieties. Besides, the GY-CDs also had C–S bonds in addition to N-containing groups, demonstrating the successful co-doping of S and N elements.

Schematic of the surface defect states and electron transition process for the synthesized B-CDs, G-CDs, and GY-CDs. Specifically, N-related surface state result in the formation of a narrow energy gap within the n–π* gap, while the S-related state generate a new energy level which lies above ground state.

(a–i) 3D fluorescence plots of GY-CDs at pH ranging from 3 to 11, the observed changing in optimal excitation and emission indicates that pH-induced different luminescent centers took the dominant place in controlling PL behaviors.

Comparative study of absorption behavior of GY-CDs between pH 3 and 11 demonstrates that the absorption band at the longer wavelength region became narrow at pH above 7, suggesting the inactivation of N-related state.

(a) Photographs of GY-CDs solutions with pH value ranging from 3 to 11 under UV light of 365 nm, the distinct change of fluorescence color between different pH ranges illustrate the pH-induced colorimetric performance of GY-CDs. Emission spectra of GY-CDs as pH increased from 3 to 11 at (b) 360 nm excitation, and (c) 460 nm excitation. (d) Ratiometric pH calibration plots of the ratio F 360/F 460 of GY-CDs as a function of pH.

The anti-interference performance of GY-CDs-based pH sensors. Effect of (a) metal ions, (b) NaCl concentration, c) thermal treatment, and (d) UV irradiation time on the ratiometric fluorescence (F 360/F 460) of GY-CDs.
Determination of pH values in real water samples by using the GY-CDs-based pH sensors.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen/sulfur Co-doping strategy to synthesis green-yellow emitting carbon dots derived from xylose: Toward application in pH sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fast-growing Tree & Agro-fibre Materials Engineering Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fast-growing Tree & Agro-fibre Materials Engineering Center, Nanjing 210037, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is still a great challenge to synthesize carbohydrate-derived carbon dots (CDs) with long-wavelength emissions via a green and facile strategy. Herein, novel xylose-derived CDs with green-yellow emission (i.e., GY-CDs) were synthesized due to the doping effect of S and N elements, using a one-pot microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The maximum emission located at 533 nm under 460 nm excitation, and the absolute quantum yield was as high as 42.44%. To unveil the emission mechanism, green and blue emissive xylose-derived CDs doped with N were also synthesized, respectively. Comparative study and various structural characterizations revealed that introducing protonated amino groups and sulfur-related moieties can narrow the energy gaps within surface states and contribute to the green-yellow emission. Further, the GY-CDs not only displayed transmutation of luminescent centers when the solution pH was above 6, but also exhibited ratiometric fluorescence changes in the pH range of 6–10 under 360 and 460 nm excitation, such distinctive dual-mode response, which together with excellent anti-interference, good water solubility, and outstanding photostability enables the prepared GY-CDs to be potential pH nanosensors.

ce:section.ce:para: The last few years have witnessed a surge of research pertaining to fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) owing to their unique chemical structure and optical properties. Unlike the conventional semiconductor quantum dots always containing toxic heavy metal elements, CDs are mainly composed of non-toxic C, O and N elements, and also possess numerously superior characteristics including outstanding photoluminescence (PL) performances, good water solubility, high photostability, ease preparation and functionalization, low/no toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. Benefiting from these merits, CDs have demonstrated great application potential in the fields of optoelectronic devices, fluorescence sensing, photocatalysis, bioimaging, drug delivery, and fluorescent ink [1–6]. Thus far, thousands of carbon sources and hundreds of synthesis methods have been reported for the preparation of CDs. With the constant-depth study, the bottom-up synthesis strategies, such as hydrothermal method, thermal decomposition and microwave irradiation has been considered to be superior in applicability and flexibility over the top-down methods including arc-discharge, laser ablation, and chemical oxidation [7–9]. Apart from developing facile synthesis strategies, an increasing interest has been focused on synthesizing CDs from carbohydrates that are commonly derived from biomass [10–16], because the mono- and polysaccharides have many great advantages, such as easy to obtain, abundant and low cost. However, this kind of biomass-based CDs always suffer from low yield synthesis, inferior luminescence efficiency, and monotonous blue emission, which are still big obstacles to their practical large-scale application. (see Table 1 )

In the view of CDs that were synthesized using citric acid, amino acid, or phenylenediamine as primary precursor, there are increasing researches have proved that functionalizing precursor with heteroatom-containing molecule or even heteroatom self-doping is an efficient way for achieving high quantum yield (QY) and long-wavelength emissions. In general, these common precursors with or without dopant are more prone to dehydration in the hydrothermal environment, and then the formed intermediate polymeride can be further in situ carbonized together with dehydrogenation reaction, which is conducive to generate efficient aromatic sp2-conjugated system containing specific chemical bonds. By research on the resulting PL properties under controlled and comparable conditions, several intrinsic factors for the tunable PL emissions of CDs have been proposed, including conjugated sp2-domain, particle size, graphite structure, degree of oxidation, nitrogen-containing functional groups, etc. [17–22]. Although the conclusions regarding PL mechanism are very variant, it is generally believed that heteroatom doping is instrumental for generating multiple defect states within CDs structures, and thus giving rise to desirable PL properties. For the carbohydrate-derived CDs, heteroatom doping, especially for the introduction of nitrogen-containing groups, can well control their local electron density, resulting in the enhancement of QY. Nonetheless, the CDs synthesized from carbohydrates coupling with dopants always emit fluorescence in blue-light region, because the band gap is hard to be narrowed through a moderate heteroatom doping approach. Motivated by this issue, a few studies have made great efforts to synthesize long-wavelength emissive CDs from carbohydrate [23–26], but the developed synthesis strategies often requires strong acid, organic solvent, complex processing, and long treatment time, making them inconvenient and unsustainable. As a matter of fact, underlying the large difficulty in controlling the emission behaviors of carbohydrate-derived CDs is heavily related to the ambiguous PL mechanism. Therefore, developing a facile strategy to synthesize carbohydrate-derived CDs with long-wavelength fluorescence as well as probe the origins of PL emission are still urgent for their value-added utilization.

According to our previous research concerning on the xylose-derived CDs, we have proposed that O-containing groups and protonated amino groups were mainly responsible for the blue and green emission, respectively [27]. Based on this, it is expected that doping S element may further narrow the band gap of the amino-functionalized green emitting CDs (G-CDs), because S has a lower electronegativity than O and N. Therefore, in the present report, novel N and S co-doped CDs were synthesized via a one-step microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, using xylose as carbon source and 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonicacid (2,4-DASA) as dopant, respectively. The synthesized green-yellow emissive CDs (GY-CDs) were characterized by primary excitation-independent PL behavior with optimal emission at 533 nm, showing red-shifted phenomenon with respect to the G-CDs. The PL mechanism of GY-CDs were investigated carefully by the characterization of their morphological, structural, and optical properties. Furthermore, since the N-, and O-related surface defect states within GY-CDs showed different changes upon doping extra proton, the pH dependency of fluorescence arising from GY-CDs demonstrated different rules at 360 and 460 nm excitations. Benefiting from this distinctive PL performance, the GY-CDs were found to be promising pH nanosensors that can assay pH range via fluorescent colorimetric changes and detect pH quantitatively as the pH value ranged from 6 to 10.

D-(+)-Xylose, 2,4-DASA, m-phenylenediamine (mPD) were all purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethanediamine (EDA), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and sodium hydrate (NaOH) were procured from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). Metal salts including CuCl2ꞏ2H2O, AlCl3ꞏ6H2O, MnCl2ꞏ4H2O, FeCl3, NaCl, and so on came from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) of different pH values were obtained from Haibiao Technologies Co., Ltd (Fuzhou, China). All reagents were used as received without further purification, and ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment.

The xylose-derived CDs were synthesized by a one-step microwave method, as seen in Fig. 1 a. Typically, 1.85 mmol of 2,4-DASA was dissolved in 10 mL of thermal water, and then 0.67 mmol of xylose was added to the solution under stirring. The obtained mixture was placed in a reacting vessel and heated in microwave reactor at 200 °C for 10 min. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained black suspension was centrifuged for 10 min at 8000 rpm, and then filtered through a PTFE syringe filter with pore size of 0.22 μm. For further purification, the collected brown solution was kept for dialysis (1000 Da) with a period of 48 h. To gain insight into the PL mechanism, typical G-CDs were also synthesized according to our previously reported method with some modifications. Briefly, 0.67 mmol of xylose, 1.85 mmol of mPD, and 200 μL of HCl was added to 10 mL of ultrapure water, the synthesis procedure of G-CDs was the same as that of GY-CDs. In addition, blue emissive xylose-derived CDs were also synthesized, the concentration of xylose and the synthetic conditions were the same as above, while the dopant was replaced with EDA (1.85 mmol). For brevity, the as-prepared blue emissive CDs were denoted as B-CDs. All the purified CDs solutions were stored at 4 °C for further use, and their solid CDs samples were also obtained by freeze-drying. The concentration of GY-CDs solution in this research was 50.4 μg/mL unless otherwise specified.

The commercialized microwave reactor was produced by Chemistry Electronic Microwave Company, (DISCOVER SP, USA). The fluorescence characterization was performed on an F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The absolute QYs were determined by using an integrating sphere attached to an FLS1000 spectrofluorometer (EI, UK). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were collected on a Lambda 950 UV–Visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). PL decay curves were measured by a FLS980 time-correlated single-photon counting system (EI, UK). JEM-2100 UHR transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) was used for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs samples. The Raman spectra were recorded using a laser confocal micro-Raman spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) by excitation of 785 nm. Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was carried out on a Vertex 80 V FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using an AXIS UltraDLD electron spectrometer (Shimadzu, UK). The energy dispersive spectra (EDS) were collected on a JSM-7600F scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan).

It can be seen from the representative TEM image in Fig. 1b, the GY-CDs were spherical in shape with an average diameter of 8.43 nm, and the sizes within the range of 5–12 nm (see the illustration at top-right of Fig. 1b). Fig. 1c shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) in which the well-defined diffraction spots and rings can be recognized, demonstrating the crystalline nature of GY-CDs. Further, the individual nanodot in the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image revealed well-resolved lattice fringes with an interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1d), which matches to the (100) facet of graphite [28]. Raman analysis was performed to further elucidate the structure of carbogenic cores. As shown in Fig. 1e, the Raman spectrum shows a sp2-hybrid G-band at 1580 cm−1 and a defect-induced D-band at 1350 cm−1, the intensity ratio I D/I G, which commonly indicates the proportion of sp3 and sp2 carbon, was determined as 0.75 for GY-CDs, implying that a large sp2-domains was formed within the GY-CDs [29]. The formation of such compact carbogenic cores could be facilitated by the high energy distribution generating from microwave irradiation in the reaction system, because the freshly formed CC bonds can be ordered as the violent reaction proceed. Further, EDS pattern, as shown in Fig. 1f, manifests the distribution of C, O, N, and S elements on GY-CDs surfaces, indicating the successful doping of S and N elements within just 10 min. Then, detailed information on surface chemical composition of GY-CDs was examined through FTIR analysis, as illustrated in Fig. 1g, the assignment of absorption peaks reveals that the GY-CDs contained O–H (3426 cm−1), amine (3230 cm−1), methylene (2933 and 2848 cm−1), CC (1630 cm−1), and aromatic C–N (1400 and 1320 cm−1). Notably, the emerging peaks at 1174 and 1068 cm−1 also suggest the presence of C–S and –SO3− within CDs structures [30,31]. The existence of these multiple groups not only resulted in a good solubility of GY-CDs in water, but is also likely to be an important factor for tuning PL behaviors.

The optical properties including excitation/emission performance, absorption behavior, fluorescence decay, and PL QY were investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the GY-CDs exhibited both excitation-dependent and -independent emission behaviors when the excitation wavelength was in the range of 360–480 nm. Specifically, as the excitation wavelength changed from 360 to 400 nm, the emission peaks showed a slight red-shifted phenomenon from 526 to 533 nm, accompanied by the increase of PL intensity, while further changing excitation cannot lead to the variation of emission peaks, suggesting a typical excitation-independent emission behavior. The PL intensity was maximum at 460 nm excitation, and then decreased gradually with the increase of excitation wavelength. Such different excitation-related PL behaviors imply that the GY-CDs had at least two luminescent centers within their structures. Furthermore, combining with the analysis on size distribution, we believe that, unlike the sp2-domain-controlled PL emission, the distinctive emission of GY-CDs should be originated from surface-defect states. By monitoring at 460 nm excitation, the absolute QY of 42.44% was observed for GY-CDs (Fig. S1, Supporting Information), demonstrating their high PL efficiency with respect to most of carbohydrate-derived CDs. Moreover, the production yield of GY-CDs was about 56%, which is beneficial for their large-scale practical application. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 2b displayed a strong absorption peak at 290 nm and an obvious visible absorption band ranging from 350 to 500 nm. According to many previous reports, the absorption in the higher-energy region was ascribed to the intrinsic state (π–π*) transition from the aromatic sp2 domains (CC and CN), while the wide absorption band that emerged in the lower-energy region was more likely to be related to the defect states induced by surface-contained functional groups [4,32]. In addition, the excitation spectrum of the GY-CDs recorded with emission wavelength at 533 nm showed a major peak at 460 nm and a shoulder peak at 410 nm. Remarkably, the peak wavelength of the PL excitation spectrum ties in well with the wide absorption band, evidencing that the green-yellow fluorescence was more likely to originate from surface defect states [33–35]. In order to confirm the possible luminescent centers within GY-CDs, the time-resolved PL spectrum monitored at 460 nm excitation was measured, as shown in Fig. 2c. Through fitting the PL decay curve based on double-exponential function, an average fluorescent lifetime of 5.71 ns was determined, the lifetime decay on the nanosecond level suggested the singlet state nature of emission of the GY-CDs [36]. Also, the fitting results reveals that the fluorescence of GY-CDs involved rapid-decay process (τ1) and slow-decay process (τ2), corresponding to the radiative transitions in core state and surface state, respectively [4]. In fact, the τ2 shared as high as 90.9%, which indicates that the luminescence center within surface state is mostly responsible for the PL emission of GY-CDs.

For better understanding the PL mechanism of GY-CDs, the comparative analysis on the optical properties of G-CDs and B-CDs were also performed. As shown in Fig. S2, the G-CDs had a maximum emission wavelength of 518 nm (under excitation at 440 nm) with dominant excitation-independent PL performance, while the typical excitation-dependent PL behavior can be observed for the B-CDs, and their optical emission was centered at 460 nm as the excitation changed to 370 nm. More intriguingly, both of the synthesized the G- and B-CDs showed considerable absolute QY, with values of 86.14 and 62.73%, respectively (as shown in Figs. S3 and S4). As seen in Fig. S5, the further characterization on absorption behaviors reveals that the G-CDs showed almost the same electronic transitions as the GY-CDs, both in the intrinsic state as well as in the surface defect state, thus implying a possible defect state emission of G-CDs. Notably, in addition to the π–π* transition with absorbing peak at 290 nm, the B-CDs presented another absorption peak at 325 nm with a tail extending to visible light region, which is commonly attributed to the n–π* transition of CO bonds [3]. Furthermore, the fluorescence excitation spectra of both G-CDs and B-CDs also overlapped with their corresponding absorption band that located at longer wavelengths, further confirming that the electron transitions occurred in the surface states were responsible their PL emissions. By analyzing the TEM image of G-CDs (Fig. S6a), it can be determined that their diameter was in the range of 3.59–8.77 nm with an average size of around 5.88 nm, which is slightly smaller than our previously synthesized G-CDs with different average diameters (6.80–9.88 nm) [27]. Therefore, it is convinced that the red-shifted emission of GY-CDs with respect to the G-CDs is free from the size controlling. Moreover, the average size of B-CDs was approximately 4.13 nm, with a narrower size distribution from 2.32 to 6.23 nm (Fig. S6b). Figs. S6c and d suggest that the synthesized two types of nanostructures with different PL also had crystal lattice as same as that in GY-CDs. Although the GY-, G-, and B-CDs synthesized from xylose demonstrated difference in major size distribution and average size, the size-independent PL emission of GY- and G-CDs indicates that the surface states rather than the size of sp2-domain played a key role in regulating PL emission of xylose-derived CDs.

To clarify the origins of different emissions arising from GY-, G-, and B-CDs, XPS analysis was performed to unveil the surface states of the synthesized CDs, and the results were presented in Fig. 3 and Fig. S7. The full spectrum of GY-CDs showed five typical peaks: C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV), O1s (532 eV), S2s (231 eV), and S2p (168 eV), suggesting a notable doping of N and S within their structures. Furthermore, the surface atomic concentration of C, N, O, and S was 65.5%, 11.8%, 18.3%, and 4.4%, respectively, as estimated by XPS elemental analysis (Fig. S7a). The high-resolution XPS of C1s obtained from GY-CDs showed three peaks at 284.5, 285.2 and 286.1 eV, which can be attributed to CC/C–C, C–N/C–S, and C–O bond, respectively [37,38]. The N1s XPS spectrum was de-convoluted into four peaks, revealing the presence of the pyridinic N (398.8 eV), pyrrolic N (399.5 eV), graphitic N (400.2 eV), and amino N (401.4 eV) within the GY-CDs [17,39]. Note that the pyridine-like and pyrrole-like N can contribute to the π-conjugated system with a pair of p-electrons, resulting in a high electron density, and thus desirable PL QY of CDs. Additionally, the content of amino N was also at a high level, implying that the GY-CDs surfaces were attached with a great number of amino groups. The O1s spectra displayed three peaks at 532.1, 533.1, and 534.0 eV, corresponding to the C–OH/C–O–C, C–O, and absorbed H2O [34,40]. In addition, the S2p spectra contained three components that can be assigned to the different types of C–S bonds, with binding energy at 167.6, 168.4 and 169.1 eV, respectively [30,41].

For the G-CDs, the XPS in the full range proved the existence of C (78.9%), N (10.3%), O (8.4%), and Cl (2.4%), the small amount of Cl element in G-CDs was related to the participation of HCl in the synthesis reaction (Fig. S7b). Considering that the synthesis strategy of G-CDs as well as their emission behavior were almost the same as G-CDs synthesized in our previous research, thus doping G-CDs with Cl element had can hardly tailor their PL emissions. In the high-resolution XPS spectra, the C1s band exhibited three peaks at 284.4, 285.2 and 286.1 eV, attributed to the C–C/CC, C–N, and C–O, respectively. From the deconvolution of the N1s spectrum, there were four N-related species can be observed, including pyridinic N (398.7 eV), pyrrolic N (399.3 eV), graphitic N (400.2 eV), and amino N (401.2 eV). Moreover, the analysis on O1s spectra manifested the presence of C–OH/C–O–C (532.1 eV) and C–O (533.2 eV) bonds in G-CDs. From the XPS survey scan, the as-prepared B-CDs were mainly composed of 68.2% C, 12.2% N, and 19.6% O elements (Fig. S7c). The typical high-resolution C1s spectra indicate that the C atoms in the B-CDs were connected in the forms of C–C/CC (284.5 eV), C–N (285.3 eV), C–O (286.2 eV), and CO (288.1 eV), respectively. The N1s band revealed the existence of pyridinic N (398.7 eV), pyrrolic N (399.5 eV), and graphitic N (400.3 eV). Additionally, the O1s band can be fitted into three peaks, which were assigned to the CO (531.1 eV), C–OH/C–O–C (532.1 eV), and C–O (533.3 eV) bonds, respectively. From the above data, the attachment of CO moieties and the absence of amino groups on the B-CDs surfaces indicated that their surface chemical state was quite different from that of the GY- and G-CDs. The surface potential of these different CDs samples from GY-CDs, G-CDs to B-CDs were determined to be 36.2, 31.8, and −26.5 mV, respectively. The positively charged surfaces for GY-CDs and G-CDs were likely due to the fact that a large amount of protonated amino groups were attached on their surfaces, whereas the negative charge of B-CDs surfaces could be mainly resulted from surface-contained negative ionized carboxyl groups.

According to the above-mentioned analysis, an energy-level model for explaining the red-shifted emission from the B-CDs, G-CDs to GY-CDs was schematically illustrated in Fig. 4 . As confirmed in earlier reports, electronic transitions in CDs are commonly in the forms of π–π* and n–π*; however, the n–π* transition is more likely to be predominant owing to the presence of CO groups on CDs surfaces [42]. Thus, this chemical species and other O-containing groups, such as C–OH and C–O–C, on B-CDs surfaces could induce multiple energy levels (O-related state) within the n–π* gap, such electronic structure within B-CDs are highly responsible for their typical excitation-dependent blue PL emission. By contrast, because numerous protonated amino groups were attached on the surfaces of G-CDs, the excited energy levels within surface state were relatively concentrated, and a narrower energy gap can be formed, resulting in that the G-CDs predominately exhibited excitation-independent emission in the green-light region. For the synthesized GY-CDs, their surfaces were also covered with plentiful amino groups but few CO bonds, thus resulting the same N-related surface state as that within G-CDs. Importantly, chemical doping S into CDs was likely to create a new energy level (S-related state) above ground state, which can further narrow the energy gap induced by protonated amino groups, and thus give rise to the excitation-independent green-yellow PL emission.

In principles, the electron transition on CDs surfaces are commonly susceptible to external proton doping because the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups have large effect on the surface state. To gain further insight into the surface state-controlled emission of GY-CDs, the PL performance of aqueous GY-CDs solution at different pH values from 3 to 11 was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 , a distinctive transmutation of luminescent center of GY-CDs can be observed from 3D fluorescence plots. In detail, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were located at 466 and 533 nm, respectively, by varying the pH value between 3 and 5, while two luminescent centers can be observed from GY-CDs as the pH increased to 6, that is, the maximum PL intensities can be obtained at emission wavelength of 533 and 522 nm under excitation at 466 and 406 nm, respectively. Interestingly, upon varying the pH value from 6 to 7, the aqueous GY-CDs showed a fluorescence emission band that continuously blue-shifted from 522 to 510 nm, while the corresponding excitation wavelength decreased from 522 to 510 nm. Further, at pH values more than 7, the luminescent center of GY-CDs remained almost unchanged. Based on the results presented above, it is reasonable to conclude that the variation in PL centers induced by pH changing highly associated with the alternated action of different surface states.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the evolution of luminescence center can be also revealed by comparing the absorption spectra of GY-CDs in the solution of pH from 3 to 11, the absorption peaked at 290 nm was relatively stable as the pH increased from 3 to 11, while the absorption band within the wavelength range of 350–500 nm became narrow when the solution pH was above 6. Based on the above results, we assumed that the N-related state induced by surface-contained protonated amino groups could suffer inactivation once the pH changed from 6 to 7, the intrinsic reason for this phenomenon was highly related to the deprotonation of amino groups. Due to the inevitable introduction of O-containing groups as the reaction proceed, such as C–OH and C–O–C groups, the resulting O-related surface state could take the dominant place in controlling the PL emission of the GY-CDs as the pH value increased to over 6.

As shown in Fig. 7 a, the colorimetric change of GY-CDs at different pH values from 3 to 11 can be easily perceived with the naked eye under UV lamp. Briefly, the aqueous GY-CDs solution at pH 3–5 emitted bright green-yellow fluorescence, while cyan in color can be clearly observed from fluorescence when the solution pH value was above 6. This colorimetric response of GY-CDs to pH was highly reversible as the pH of the solution was cycled in the range of 3–11. To explore the reason for the transformation of fluorescence color, the pH-dependent emission behaviors of GY-CDs obtained at 360 and 460 nm excitations were thoroughly investigated, as depicted in Fig. 7b and c. Impressively, the fluorescence emission peaks monitored at 360 nm excitation were clearly blue-shifted from 526 to 514 nm when the solution pH increased from 3 to 6, while the GY-CDs solution in the pH region of 7–11 showed a fixed emission with peak wavelength of 506 nm. The significant blue-shift of emission peak within different pH ranges was the intrinsic reason for generating different luminescent colors under UV light, which is beneficial to distinguishing pH region by naked eye. Furthermore, it is quite evident that the pH-dependent emission spectra obtained under excitation at 460 nm displayed a gradual blue-shift of PL peak from 533 to 518 nm. In addition to the variation of emission peak, the PL intensities of GY-CDs at pH 3–11 also showed different dependency relationships when the excitation wavelength shifted from 360 to 460 nm. In specific, with increasing pH value from 3 to 5, the PL intensities kept almost unchanged both under excitation at 360 and 460 nm; however, the PL intensities obtained at 360 nm excitation exhibited a sharp increase when the pH was elevated from 6 to 8, and then followed by a slow increase as the pH further increased to 10. Finally, the fluorescence was weakened slightly when the solution pH was changed from 10 to 11. By contrast, an inverse relationship can be observed from the variation of PL intensity recorded at 460 nm excitation when the solution pH increased from 6 to 10. The different dependencies of PL intensity on pH variation in the region of 6–10 are compelling and have prompted us to explore their potential in the pH detection. Fig. 7d shows the plot of F 360/F 460 versus pH values, F 360 and F 460 represent the PL intensities obtained at 360 and 460 nm excitation, respectively. Notably, the ratio of F 360 to F 460 was stable in the pH range between 3 and 5, while further increasing pH from 6 to 10, the average value of F 360/F 460 exhibited a linear relationship with pH that can be fitted as y = 2.64x−15.65. The significant changes of ratiometric fluorescence indicated that the proposed pH nanosensors were bestowed with excellent sensitivity.

The probable PL quenching effect of various cations on GY-CDs was investigated, as shown in Fig. 8 a. Different metal ions including Cu2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Cr2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Al3+, and Mn2+ of each at concentration of 50 mM were added to the GY-CDs solution with pH value of 5. In comparison with control sample, the ratio of F 360 to F 460 obtained from each CDs solution in the presence of metal ion showed negligible variation. In addition, the fluorescence of GY-CDs at pH = 7 was also highly resistant to high-concentration salt solution (Fig. 8b). The above data demonstrate that using GY-CDs for pH sensing are free from the interference of ionic strength and metal cation. Considering that the thermal stability of GY-CDs is important for pH sensing, the aqueous GY-CDs solution without a further adjustment of pH value was subjected to thermal treatment, and the measured F 360/F 460 exhibited negligible fluctuation when the treatment temperature was increased from 0 to 100 °C (Fig. 8c). Moreover, the PL stability of these GY-CDs was examined by continuously irradiating them with a UV lamp for 8 h, as depicted in Fig. 8d, the almost unchanged value of F 360/F 460 indicated the high photostability of GY-CDs.

To verify the feasibility of the GY-CDs-based pH sensors in practical pH detection, a series of tap water samples at different pH values, which were adjusted by using NaOH and HCl, were prepared, then the GY-CDs were dissolved into the above each sample with a final concentration of 50.4 μg/mL. Benefiting from the dual excitation model, excellent anti-interference, and good water solubility of GY-CDs, the detection results obtained from the present analytical mode were comparable to those determined by pH meter, thus demonstrating that the GY-CDs-based pH sensors are accurate and reliable in practical pH sensing.

In summary, we report a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal method to synthesize S, N co-doped CDs with green-yellow fluorescence using low-cost xylose as carbon source. For disclosing the underlying reason for the long-wavelength emission, another two types of xylose-derived CDs with typical blue and green PL emissions were also synthesized as controls. Comparatively studied focused on the morphological structure, size distribution, chemical composition, and surface chemical state revealed that the tunable emission from xylose-derived B-CDs, G-CDs, to GY-CDs were heavily dependent on the change of surface states. In our case, multiple energy levels induced by O-containing groups took the dominant place due to the existence of surface-contained CO moieties, which contributed to the PL emission with typical excitation-dependent behaviors for B-CDs. Attributed to the attachment of plentiful protonated amino groups on CDs surfaces, this N-related surface state induced a relatively narrow energy gap and then predominantly controlled PL emissions of G-CDs with excitation-independent characteristic. In particular, further doping S in the xylose-derived CDs with surface-contained protonated amino groups can raise the ground state and narrow the energy gap induced by N-related state, thus resulting in that the maximum emission of GY-CDs was red-shifted compared with that of G-CDs. Since the N-related and O-related surface defect states displayed different response to pH variation, the pH-dependent PL arising from GY-CDs showed great difference not only in the variation of PL intensity, but also in the change of emission wavelength. Further investigation demonstrates that the ratio of PL intensities obtained at 360 and 460 nm excitation was linearly changed over the pH range of 6–10, such ratiometric fluorescence changing coupled with excellent anti-interference, good water solubility, outstanding photostability, and fluorescent colorimetric response manifest that the synthesized GY-CDs are potential nanosensors for practical pH sensing.

Pei Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Ziqi Zhu: Investigation. Weimin Chen: Writing - review & editing. Min Luo: Investigation. Wei Zhang: Investigation. Tao Zhang: Investigation. Minzhi Chen: Conceptualization. Xiaoyan Zhou: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
